params.text:
Person-centred care, duty of care and rights

Person-centred care means placing the individual at the heart of everything you do. Instead of fitting people into a routine, you find out what matters to each person — their preferences, history, culture and goals — and support them to have choice and control over their own life.

Your duty of care is your legal and moral responsibility to keep the people you support safe and to act in their best interests, while still respecting their right to make their own decisions. Duty of care sits alongside people’s rights, including the right to:

  • Be treated with dignity and respect
  • Be involved in decisions about their own care
  • Have their confidentiality respected — personal information is only shared on a need-to-know basis, in line with data protection law, unless there is a genuine risk of harm

These principles are not optional extras. They are the foundation of safe, respectful, high-quality care.

params.question: Which of the following best describes person-centred care?

answers.text: Building support around each individual's own preferences, goals and choices.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Person-centred care puts the individual, their choices and what matters to them at the centre.

answers.text: Giving everyone exactly the same routine so care is consistent for staff.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A one-size-fits-all routine is the opposite of person-centred care.

answers.text: Making all decisions on the person's behalf to keep things simple.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. This removes the person's choice and control, which person-centred care is designed to protect.

answers.text: Focusing only on physical tasks and ignoring emotional needs.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Person-centred care considers the whole person, including emotional, social and cultural needs.
